metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `run_workflow` as a fourth entry in the classification override table: WRITE-registrar but also call-time `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`-gated (when the workflow has LLM steps and `dry_run=False`), and specify how the generator/annotation should represent this conditional dual gate.
findings.finding: The plan's 'closed 3-entry special-case override table' omits run_workflow, which lives in the WRITE registrar but also has a call-time REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM gate (when the workflow has LLM steps and dry_run=False). The plan asserts the table is closed/complete, but the code shows a fourth hybrid case. A generator following the plan's classification table would silently annotate run_workflow as purely REBAR_MCP_READONLY-gated, producing a misleading reference entry.
findings.location: Source of truth section — Classification rule table; also Scope section ('three special cases are inline ANNOTATIONS')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the override table with a row: `run_workflow` -> `REBAR_MCP_READONLY` (registration) + conditional `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM` at call-time (for LLM-step workflows); annotate in the Write-gated section as 'also requires REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM for workflows with LLM steps (dry_run=False)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the reconcile annotation spec to include BOTH gates for mutating modes: 'live/mutating modes require REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC=1 AND are blocked by REBAR_MCP_READONLY' (the code checks readonly first, then jira_sync).
findings.finding: The plan's annotation for `reconcile` specifies only `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC` as the live-mode gate, but the code gates mutating modes on BOTH `_readonly()` AND `_allow_jira_sync()` (readonly is checked first). A generator following the plan will annotate reconcile as 'live mode gated by REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC' without mentioning that REBAR_MCP_READONLY also blocks mutating modes — leaving users incorrectly believing they can run live reconcile on a readonly server if JIRA_SYNC is set.
findings.location: Source of truth section — Classification rule table, READS row; AC item 3 ('reconcile row text contains REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC')
findings.suggested_fix: Change the override table entry to: `reconcile` -> annotated 'mutating modes gated by both `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC` and `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`; dry-run/check modes always available'.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the held-out unit test file (e.g. `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py`) to the Files deliverable list and name it explicitly in AC3 so the implementer has a clear, unambiguous target.
findings.finding: AC3 requires a 'held-out unit test' that asserts section-bounded placement of all three special cases (sign_review, reconcile, fsck) using section-slicing as an independent oracle — but this test file is not listed in the Files deliverable at the bottom of the plan, leaving the implementer without a clear target file to create.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 vs. Files section (bottom of plan)
findings.suggested_fix: Append `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py` (new) to the Files list and update AC3's verify description to reference it by path, e.g. 'the assertions are in `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py` and covered by `make test`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the FastMCP introspection API the generator will use to enumerate registered tools (e.g., whether to access `mcp._tool_manager.tools`, call `asyncio.run(mcp.list_tools())`, or use another stable surface), and note the version-coupling risk of any private attribute access.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify which FastMCP API to use for extracting registered tool metadata (names + descriptions) after calling each registrar onto its own FastMCP instance. FastMCP stores tools in internal structures (e.g., `_tool_manager.tools` dict or an async `list_tools()` coroutine) — neither of which is a documented stable public API. Using a private attribute creates a version-coupling risk; using an async path requires an event loop. The gen_env_registry.py pattern avoids this entirely by using stdlib AST. The plan says 'read each tool's name + first-line summary (from .description)' without stating the access path, leaving the implementer to discover potentially unstable FastMCP internals.
findings.location: Scope section: 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP ... read each tool's name + first-line summary (from .description)'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that the generator accesses `mcp._tool_manager.tools` (a dict keyed by tool name with a `.description` attribute on each tool object), or alternatively that it uses `asyncio.run(mcp.list_tools())` to get `Tool` objects with `.name` and `.description`. Note that if FastMCP's internals change, the generator must be updated. A more resilient alternative is an AST scan of the registrar files for nested function definitions under `@mcp.tool(...)` decorators, extracting name + docstring — this has zero third-party API coupling.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope whether `gen_mcp_reference.py` calls `build_server()` once (inspecting tool names post-registration against the closed classification table) or calls each registrar on a separate FastMCP with an explicitly-constructed `ctx` — and if the latter, document that `ctx` must include `MODE_CAPS`/`Mode` from `_load_engine_mode()` (or a stub equivalent).
findings.finding: The plan's Scope asserts the generator will 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP' by calling each registrar function independently, but building the required `ctx` SimpleNamespace outside `build_server()` is non-trivial: `ctx` carries `MODE_CAPS` and `Mode` populated by `_load_engine_mode()` (which loads the bundled rebar_reconciler engine binary from disk via file-path import) and several live gate closures. The plan does not resolve whether the generator will call `build_server()` directly (collapsing the per-registrar boundary) or reconstruct `ctx` independently (requiring it to replicate `_load_engine_mode()`). This is an unverified capability claim — the path works, but the concrete implementation strategy is unspecified and either branch has real consequences for correctness (combined build_server() gives correct 47-tool output but makes per-registrar attribution dependent on which registrar registered a given tool, not on the generator's own call).
findings.location: Scope section — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0'
findings.suggested_fix: The simpler and correct path is: call `build_server()` once with `REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0` (so all 47 tools register), then classify each returned tool by name using the closed 3-entry table. This avoids reconstructing `ctx` and eliminates the per-registrar-instance complexity. Update the Scope to say 'call build_server() with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0, then classify by name via the closed table' rather than 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0` is set in an isolated scope (e.g., `os.environ` patched with a context manager and restored after each registrar call, or the registrar enumeration run in a subprocess) so the env mutation cannot leak to other tests or imports.
findings.finding: The plan requires setting `REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0` to force write-tool registration, but does not specify whether this is done via in-process `os.environ` mutation or via a subprocess/isolated environment. In-process mutation leaks the env var to any other code running in the same process (e.g., if the generator is imported in the test suite or run alongside other tests via pytest-xdist), potentially causing write tools to register in contexts that expect read-only mode.
findings.location: Scope section — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with `REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0`'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each registrar call with `with unittest.mock.patch.dict(os.environ, {'REBAR_MCP_READONLY': '0'}):` or run the enumeration in a subprocess and parse stdout, ensuring the parent process environment is never mutated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `--check` mode emits a human-readable diff (or at minimum a descriptive error message naming the first differing line) to stderr when drift is detected, so CI output is self-explanatory without manual investigation.
findings.finding: The --check failure mode is not specified to emit actionable output. A developer or CI operator seeing a red 'drift' step has no way to know what changed (which tools were added/removed, which descriptions changed) without manually diffing the files themselves.
findings.location: AC: 'Drift gate works' — `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py --check >/dev/null 2>&1`
findings.suggested_fix: In --check mode, compute the expected content, diff against the committed file with `difflib.unified_diff`, print the diff to stderr, then exit non-zero — the AC test can still use `>/dev/null 2>&1` to suppress it while the real CI step omits the redirect.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the shared `_FakeMcp` / stub-FastMCP fixture into a shared test utility (e.g. `tests/unit/conftest.py` or `tests/_mcp_stubs.py`) before adding a third inline copy in the new `gen_mcp_reference` unit test.
findings.finding: The plan's AC-3 held-out unit test will need to instantiate a fake/stub FastMCP to invoke registrars directly (the same pattern already used in test_plan_review_explain.py:19 and test_read_freshness_parity.py:165), creating a third identical inline copy of _FakeMcp and crossing the Rule-of-Three threshold with no shared fixture.
findings.location: AC line 3 ('a held-out unit test asserts, using section-bounded slicing…')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `make_fake_mcp()` factory or a `FakeMcp` class to `tests/unit/conftest.py` (or a small `tests/_mcp_stubs.py` module) and import it in `test_plan_review_explain.py`, `test_read_freshness_parity.py`, and the new `test_gen_mcp_reference.py`. The implementation is trivial (the existing inline version is already the canonical form).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/README.md` to the plan's file list and include an entry for `docs/mcp-reference.md` under the appropriate audience section (Operator or Agent).
findings.finding: docs/README.md is an unenumerated consumer that the plan does not mention updating. It is a curated, audience-grouped index of every file in docs/ — including an explicit subsection for env-vars.md (Operator section) and architecture.md (Contributor section). Adding docs/mcp-reference.md without a corresponding entry in docs/README.md leaves the index stale, and the file would be undiscoverable by audience navigation.
findings.location: Scope / Files section of the plan
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'docs/README.md' to the 'Files' list in Scope, and under the Operator or Agent section of docs/README.md add: '- **[mcp-reference.md](mcp-reference.md)** — generated reference of all 47 MCP tools, classified by gate tier (always-available / LLM-gated / write-gated); kept in sync by a CI drift gate.'

findings.finding: The AC4 count threshold `>= 47` is susceptible to silent under-counting because markdown table header rows (one per section, 4 sections) match the same grep pattern `^| ?[backtick]?[a-z_]+[backtick]? ` as tool rows, inflating the real tool count by up to 4. If the generator emits only 43 tool rows but 4 header rows (e.g., `| tool | description | gate |`), the check still passes at 47, hiding a missing-write-tools bug.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 count proof command
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the generator's `--check` output a line like `Tools: 47` and assert on that structured output: `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py --check 2>&1 | grep -qE 'Tools: 47' || echo 'COUNT WRONG'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md section (or docs/adr/) documenting the registration-time vs call-time gating architecture, the override table rationale, and how to extend it when a new hybrid tool is added.
findings.finding: The registration-time vs call-time gating distinction and the 3-entry special-case override table are novel architectural rules baked into `gen_mcp_reference.py` with no corresponding CLAUDE.md or ADR entry. A future contributor adding a new hybrid tool (e.g., another LLM-registrar tool that is write-gated) has no discoverable record of WHY the override table exists or how to extend it correctly.
findings.location: Classification rule section / `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` (new)
findings.suggested_fix: In CLAUDE.md (or a new `docs/adr/mcp-tool-gating.md`), add a short section: 'MCP tool gate classification: tools are classified by registrar (reads/llm/writes). Three tools override the default — see the override table in scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py. When adding a hybrid tool, update BOTH the registrar AND the override table.' Reference this from the generator script's module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `gen_mcp_reference.py` writes to a temporary file and atomically renames it into place (or truncates only after successful generation) so a crashed run never leaves `docs/mcp-reference.md` in a partially-written state.
findings.finding: The generator script writes docs/mcp-reference.md with no mention of atomic write (write-to-temp-then-rename). If the process is interrupted mid-write (OOM kill, SIGINT), the file is left partially written; the next --check run sees a corrupt file and fails with a confusing diff rather than a clean regeneration signal.
findings.location: Scope section — 'emit docs/mcp-reference.md'; AC drift-gate check
findings.suggested_fix: Generate content into a `tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile` in the same directory, then `os.replace(tmp.name, 'docs/mcp-reference.md')` — atomic on POSIX, safe on Windows with the same-filesystem guarantee.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale explaining why live registrar introspection (calling each `register_*_tools` onto a dedicated FastMCP instance) is preferred over static AST scanning — specifically that it uses the SAME registration-time gate logic as production, ensuring the documented gate classification can never drift from the actual behavior.
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for why live registrar introspection (calling each registrar onto a real FastMCP instance) was chosen over the structurally different alternative of static AST scanning of the registrar files (as gen_env_registry.py does for env vars). The absence of a stated rationale leaves the implementer without guidance on whether the tradeoff — import-time side effects and third-party API coupling vs. simpler static extraction — was considered.
findings.location: Scope section / What section — rationale for approach is absent
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'The live-registrar approach is chosen over AST scanning because it exercises the exact same `@mcp.tool()` registration paths as the production server — including the `ctx.readonly()` early-return guard in `register_write_tools` — so the documented gate classification cannot silently diverge from the actual registration behavior if a registrar is refactored.'

findings.finding: AC3's verification for special-case placement is 'Verify by reading the generated sections' — a manual human-inspection step, not a concrete deterministic proving command. The three special cases (`sign_review` in write-gated not llm-gated, `reconcile` annotated jira-gated, `fsck` readonly-gated) are the highest-risk classification decisions in the plan, yet they have no shell-executable check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3

findings.finding: The plan's Scope section describes FOUR output sections ('Read-only (always)', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated', and 'the jira-gated reconcile live mode'), but AC line 2's shell verification only tests for THREE section labels ('Read-only', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated'). It is unspecified whether the jira-gated reconcile content is a standalone fourth section heading, a subsection of Read-only, or an inline annotation — leaving the generator's structural output protocol ambiguous.
findings.location: Scope (four-section description) vs. AC line 2 (three-section grep check)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `'jira-gated'` or `'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC'` as a fourth term in the AC line 2 grep, OR (b) reword the Scope to say 'three sections, with reconcile annotated in-line within the Read-only section' — pick one and make the AC match.

findings.finding: The AC4 count-proof command embeds literal backtick characters inside a bash double-quoted `$(...)` expansion. In bash, backticks inside `$(...)` are still interpreted as command substitution, so the grep pattern `'^| ?`?[a-z_]+`? '` will trigger a nested command substitution on the fragment `?[a-z_]+` rather than being passed literally to grep, causing the command to error or silently produce a wrong count — the proving command silently lies.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 count proof command
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the double-quoted grep pattern with a single-quoted one to prevent backtick interpolation:
```bash
python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py && [ "$(grep -cE '^[|] ?[`]?[a-z_]+[`]? ' docs/mcp-reference.md)" -ge 47 ] || echo "TOO FEW TOOLS"
```

findings.finding: AC3's verification of correct special-case placement (`sign_review` in write-gated tier, `reconcile` annotated as jira-gated, `fsck` readonly-gated) is specified as '(Verify by reading the generated sections)' — a manual step — with no automated assertion. The classification override table is the most non-trivial logic in the generator; a regression that misclassifies these tools would pass all CI checks undetected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py classification logic
findings.suggested_fix:
Extend AC3 with runnable shell assertions, for example:
```
grep -A 60 'Write-gated' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -qE 'sign_review' || echo 'MISCLASSIFIED sign_review'
grep -E 'reconcile' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC' || echo 'MISSING jira annotation on reconcile'
grep -E 'fsck' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'REBAR_MCP_READONLY' || echo 'MISSING readonly annotation on fsck'
```
These can live in the AC shell block or as parametrized assertions in `tests/unit/test_gen_mcp_reference.py`.

findings.finding: The Scope names FOUR output sections ('Read-only (always)', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated', and a fourth jira-gated reconcile section), but AC2's shell verify only checks for THREE section labels ('Read-only', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated'). An implementer could omit the fourth section and pass all ACs while violating the Scope.
findings.location: Scope (four sections) vs AC2 (three-section grep)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2's loop: `for s in 'Read-only' 'LLM-gated' 'Write-gated' 'Jira'; do grep -qi "$s" docs/mcp-reference.md || echo "NO SECTION $s"; done`

findings.finding: AC3's verification of the most logic-rich part of the generator — the three-entry special-case override table (sign_review in write-gated tier, reconcile annotated jira-gated, fsck readonly-gated) — is left as a manual 'Verify by reading the generated sections' step with no automated assertion in make test or pytest. If the classification logic is wrong, CI stays green.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3: 'The three special cases are correctly placed/annotated … (Verify by reading the generated sections.)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_mcp_reference.py`, run `gen_mcp_reference.py` (or call its public `generate()` function directly), read the output string, and use `assert` to verify section membership and annotation text for each of the three special-case tools. Parametrize for clarity.

findings.finding: The special-case placement AC relies solely on a manual human inspection oracle ('Verify by reading the generated sections') for the most error-prone classification rule (`sign_review` in write-gated tier despite being in the LLM registrar). No deterministic shell command or automated assertion verifies this placement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — item 3: 'Verify by reading the generated sections.'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add three grep-based AC commands:
```sh
# sign_review must appear under the Write-gated section, not LLM-gated
python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py
awk '/Write-gated/,/^##/' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'sign_review' || echo 'FAIL: sign_review not in write-gated section'
# reconcile must be annotated with REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC
grep -q 'reconcile.*REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC\|REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC.*reconcile' docs/mcp-reference.md || echo 'FAIL: reconcile jira annotation missing'
# fsck must appear in the read-only section with readonly annotation
awk '/Read-only/,/^##/' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'fsck' || echo 'FAIL: fsck not in read-only section'
```

findings.finding: The output section count is inconsistently specified: the Scope says FOUR labeled sections (Read-only, LLM-gated, Write-gated, and a separate jira-gated reconcile section), but the AC verification command checks only THREE section names ('Read-only', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated'). The plan never resolves whether the jira-gated reconcile live mode is a standalone fourth section or an inline annotation within the Read-only section — leaving the generator's output structure decision rule ambiguous.
findings.location: Scope ('FOUR clearly-labeled sections') vs. AC bullet 2 (grep checks only 3: 'Read-only', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope, change 'FOUR clearly-labeled sections' to 'THREE clearly-labeled sections, with reconcile annotated within the Read-only section as jira-gated (REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC) for its live mode.' This aligns with AC bullet 3's 'annotated' language and keeps reconcile structurally in the Read-only section (where it is registered) while calling out its live-mode gate.

findings.finding: The plan declares FOUR output sections but AC2's shell verification only checks for THREE section labels, and the fourth section's structural form is ambiguous — the plan alternately describes it as a separate section ('jira-gated reconcile live mode') and as an annotation within the Read-only section. No output schema for how multi-gate tools (reconcile: always listed, but REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC for live modes) are rendered in the table is specified.
findings.location: Scope section (four sections) / AC2 shell verification

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
